author: anon-ab6221f8
content:
Plan — fix my bad take from earlier. Local-fs discovery IS cheap: crosslink-enabled repos have a .crosslink/ directory at the repo root (and/or a crosslink/hub branch in the git remote — but local signal alone is enough and works offline). The prior 'can't tell which' was wrong; it was conflating 'scan the whole filesystem' with 'check a sensible list of dev roots'.

CLI surface: new subcommand
  crosslink dashboard discover [--root <PATH>]... [--depth <N>] [--track]

Behaviour:
- Walk each --root (default: $HOME) up to --depth directories deep (default 4).
- Skip obvious noise dirs: .git inside, node_modules, target, dist, .venv, __pycache__, .cache, Library, AppData, any dir starting with '.' except .crosslink itself.
- For every dir that is (a) a git repo root and (b) has .crosslink/ present, record a hit.
- Derive slug from origin remote URL; fall back to 'local/<basename>' for remote-less repos.
- Print hits as a table: SLUG / PATH / TRACKED?
- With --track, track every hit that isn't already tracked.

Reuses crate::dashboard::projects::track for the tracking path so the derivation logic stays identical. No GitHub round-trip — purely local-fs.

Tests: a temp-dir fixture mimicking a nested layout with 3 crosslink repos + 2 non-crosslink git repos + one node_modules; asserts exactly the 3 hits found, respects --depth bound, handles symlinks defensively (don't follow).
